title: Royal icing flooding
subtitle: Outline and flood method for flat-iced biscuits
excerpt: Two consistencies of royal icing: a stiff outline piped to define edges, then a thinned flood icing poured to fill. The technique used for decorated biscuits and plaques, producing a perfectly level, hard surface.
sourceNotes: Royal icing (icing sugar, egg white, and lemon juice) has been documented as the standard coating for formal cakes and biscuits since the eighteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives royal icing recipes for wedding and Christmas cakes. The two-stage outline-and-flood method is a development of that tradition, widely described in domestic cookery writing from the mid-twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Royal icing dries out quickly. Cover bowls with a damp cloth when not in use. Outline icing keeps for 24 hours covered in the fridge; flood icing for 48 hours.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard-setting icing made from icing sugar beaten with egg white (or meringue powder) and lemon juice. Sets rigid at room temperature to a smooth, matte finish. Used for formal cake coating, flooding biscuits, piping fine detail, and assembling gingerbread structures. The consistency is adjusted by adding water (to thin for flooding) or more icing sugar (to stiffen for piping).

content.text: The outline-and-flood method uses two consistencies of

: a stiff outline that holds its shape when piped and forms a wall around the biscuit edge, and a thinned flood icing that is poured inside the outline and levels itself flat. The outline must dry for at least 30 minutes before flooding, or the flood icing will push through the wall.

Test flood consistency with the 10-second rule: drag a knife through the icing and count to ten. If the surface has healed smooth by ten, it's right. Faster than 10 seconds means it's too thin and will run off the outline; slower means it won't level and will leave ridges.
Make the icing and split the consistency

Beat the {{egg-whites}} lightly with the {{lemon-juice}}. Add the {{icing-sugar}} and beat with a hand mixer for 5-7 minutes until the icing is bright white, smooth, and holds a stiff peak. This is the outline consistency. Transfer about one-third into a small bowl and cover with a damp cloth. Thin the remaining two-thirds by adding water, a few drops at a time, until it passes the 10-second test: this is the flood icing.

Fill a piping bag fitted with a small round nozzle (no. 1 or no. 2) with the stiff outline icing. Pipe a continuous line around the edge of each biscuit, just inside the rim. Keep the nozzle slightly above the surface and let the icing fall into place rather than dragging it: dragging pulls the line. Leave the outlines to dry for at least 30 minutes.

Fill a second piping bag (no nozzle, or a squeeze bottle) with the flood icing. Pipe or pour a pool of flood icing inside the outline on each biscuit. Use a toothpick or the tip of a skewer to guide the flood icing to the corners and edges. Leave to dry completely at room temperature: at least 6-8 hours for a dry surface, 24 hours for a fully hard set that can be stacked.
